basis.reasoning:
AssessmentReasoning:
- The field_answer provides a comprehensive verdict, ROI ranking, mechanism details, and implementation criteria in line with the user query. It also attempts to separate verified facts, vendor claims, and inferences.
- However, many of the field_answer's key assertions (e.g., exact competitive relationships, direct/adjacent/supplier/channel classifications, and ROI rankings tied to zero-revenue monetization) rely on interpretations rather than explicit primary-source citations. The excerpts provided include primary-source-like items (Cursor/Together AI case reads, Decagon latency claims, Hedra cost/speed moves, Cloudflare control-plane patterns, Stripe entitlements), but they do not explicitly confirm every load-bearing claim in the field_answer (e.g., that Cursor/Decagon/Hedra are not direct competitors, or that the closest analog is Cloudflare Durable Objects, or the specific ROI ranks).
- The Excerpts list does include: Decagon <400ms latency with Together AI, Cursor partnership, Hedra cost/speed improvements, Cloudflare Durable Objects reference pattern, Fly.io, WorkOS, Stripe references. These can support portions of the narrative, particularly around latency claims and architectural analogies, but they do not on their own certify all field_answer assertions.
- Therefore: Completeness should be true (the answer addresses the requested elements: verdict, ROI ranking, vendor/competitor analysis, acceptance criteria, what not to build, and citations structure) even if some parts would benefit from more explicit, line-item primary citations. Supported should be false (or at least not confidently true) because the field_answer’s specific load-bearing conclusions aren’t fully backed by the excerpts alone.
- Recommendation: To improve support, map each load-bearing conclusion to a specific excerpt with a direct citation, and clearly label inference vs. verified fact where primary sources are ambiguous.

excerpts:
Learn how Hedra moved its viral AI video models to Together AI, cutting infrastructure costs by 60%, tripling inference speed, and scaling 300x.
About Hedra
Founded by PhD researcher Michael Lingelbach, the company created Character-1, a video generation model that powers viral content across TikTok and Instagram while serving enterprise customers incorporating AI-generated media into content strategies.
Hedra builds AI that creates video content from simple prompts.
Hedra, the AI startup behind [viral baby podcasts](https://www.instagram.com/reel/DKKijabAz2L/), needed infrastructure that could scale instantly to handle viral AI-video demand without ballooning costs or tying up engineers.
Operating as both a product company and applied AI research laboratory, Hedra serves millions of videos monthly through their web platform and API access.
Since seed stage, the company's compute needs have grown 300x while maintaining consistent performance and cost efficiency at massive scale.
Hedra Scales Viral AI Video Generation with 60% Cost Savings
Hedra's models require specialized deployment infrastructure unavailable from traditional cloud providers.
Partnering with Together AI, the team shifted video inference to optimized H100/H200 clusters with kernel optimizations and flexible autoscaling.
The migration cut cloud costs by 60%, tripled inference speed, supported 300× growth in compute demand, and removed the need for extra platform engineers, preserving healthy margins as usage soared.
Hedra
citations.title: How Hedra Scales Viral AI Video Generation with 60% ...

citations.title: Control and data plane architectural pattern for Durable Objects

excerpts:
(last verified: 2026-07-10) `ctx.waitUntil()` extends the lifetime of your Worker, allowing you to perform work without blocking returning a response, and that may continue after a response is returned. It accepts a `Promise`, which the Workers runtime will continue executing, even after a response has been returned by the Worker's [handler](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/handlers/).
Do not use `waitUntil()` to keep a Durable Object alive during normal request or RPC handling. Durable Objects remain active while they are handling requests, RPC calls, response streams, WebSockets, or pending I/O. [DurableObjectState.waitUntil()](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/api/state/#waituntil) exists for API compatibility and is not needed for this behavior.
DurableObjectState.waitUntil() exists for API compatibility and is not needed for this behavior.

Overview · Cloudflare Durable Objects docs
A Durable Object is a special kind of Cloudflare Worker which uniquely combines compute with storage. Like a Worker, a Durable Object is automatically provisioned geographically close to where it is first requested, starts up quickly when needed, and shuts down when idle.
citations.title: Overview · Cloudflare Durable Objects docs
